title: Dependency-Aware Parallel Decoding via Attention for Diffusion LLMs
summary: Parallel decoding for diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) is difficult because each denoising step provides only token-wise marginal distributions, while unmasking multiple tokens simultaneously requires accounting for inter-token dependencies. We propose Dependency-Aware Parallel Decoding (DAPD), a simple, training-free decoding method that uses self-attention to induce a conditional dependency graph over masked tokens. At each iteration, edges in this graph capture strong token interactions, while non-edges indicate weak dependence. Parallel decoding is then reduced to selecting an independent set on the graph and unmasking the selected tokens in parallel. This avoids co-updating strongly coupled tokens without auxiliary models or retraining. Experiments on LLaDA and Dream show that DAPD improves the accuracy-steps trade-off over existing methods and enables more globally distributed parallel updates that better exploit the any-order generation capability of dLLMs.

processed.summary:
• DAPD introduces a **training-free decoding strategy** for **Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs)** that addresses the challenge of inter-token dependencies during parallel denoising.
• The method leverages **self-attention weights** to construct a **conditional dependency graph**, identifying which masked tokens can be safely updated in parallel.
• By selecting an **independent set** within the graph, the system avoids conflicting updates between strongly coupled tokens, enhancing generation quality.
• Evaluation on **LLaDA** and **Dream** models demonstrates a superior accuracy-steps trade-off compared to traditional greedy or random parallel decoding methods.
key_results: Proposed DAPD as a plug-and-play, training-free method for dLLMs.
Utilized self-attention to model inter-token dependencies dynamically during generation.
Reduced parallel decoding complexity to an independent set selection problem on a graph.
Improved generation quality on LLaDA and Dream benchmarks without auxiliary models.
Enabled more globally distributed parallel updates that exploit any-order generation capability.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DAPD to improve diffusion LLM decoding by using attention-based dependency graphs for safer parallel token updates.

title: Route Fragmentation Based on Resource-centric Prioritisation for Efficient Multi-Robot Path Planning in Agricultural Environments
summary: Agricultural environments present high proportions of spatially dense navigation bottlenecks for long-term navigation and operational planning of agricultural mobile robots. The existing agent-centric multi-robot path planning (MRPP) approaches resolve conflicts from the perspective of agents, rather than from the resources under contention. Further, the density of such contentions limits the capabilities of spatial interleaving, a concept that many planners rely on to achieve high throughput. In this work, two variants of the priority-based Fragment Planner (FP) are presented as resource-centric MRPP algorithms that leverage route fragmentation to enable partial route progression and limit the impact of binary-based waiting. These approaches are evaluated in lifelong simulation over a 3.6km topological map representing a commercial polytunnel environment. Their performances are contrasted against 5 baseline algorithms with varying robotic fleet sizes. The Fragment Planners achieved significant gains in throughput compared with Prioritised Planning (PP) and Priority-Based Search (PBS) algorithms. They further demonstrated a task throughput of 95% of the optimal task throughput over the same time period. This work shows that, for long-term deployment of agricultural robots in corridor-dominant agricultural environments, resource-centric MRPP approaches are a necessity for high-efficacy operational planning.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **Fragment Planner (FP)**, a resource-centric **Multi-Robot Path Planning (MRPP)** method designed for environments with high navigation bottlenecks.
* Leverages **route fragmentation** to allow agents to execute partial paths, mitigating the inefficiencies of binary-based waiting in narrow corridors.
* Shifts from traditional agent-centric conflict resolution to **resource-centric prioritisation**, enabling higher throughput in spatially constrained agricultural maps.
* Demonstrates significant scalability in **lifelong simulations**, effectively managing fleet coordination in complex commercial polytunnel environments.
key_results: Fragment Planners achieved significant throughput gains compared to Prioritised Planning (PP) and Priority-Based Search (PBS).
Reached 95% of the optimal task throughput in a large-scale 3.6km topological map simulation.
Successfully resolved spatially dense navigation bottlenecks that typically limit standard spatial interleaving.
Proven efficacy in long-term deployment scenarios for agricultural mobile robots.
Demonstrated that resource-centric approaches are superior to agent-centric models for corridor-dominant environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Fragment Planner, using route fragmentation to achieve 95% optimal throughput in complex multi-robot environments.

title: Test-Time Attention Purification for Backdoored Large Vision Language Models
summary: Despite the strong multimodal performance, large vision-language models (LVLMs) are vulnerable during fine-tuning to backdoor attacks, where adversaries insert trigger-embedded samples into the training data to implant behaviors that can be maliciously activated at test time. Existing defenses typically rely on retraining backdoored parameters (e.g., adapters or LoRA modules) with clean data, which is computationally expensive and often degrades model performance. In this work, we provide a new mechanistic understanding of backdoor behaviors in LVLMs: the trigger does not influence prediction through low-level visual patterns, but through abnormal cross-modal attention redistribution, where trigger-bearing visual tokens steal attention away from the textual context - a phenomenon we term attention stealing. Motivated by this, we propose CleanSight, a training-free, plug-and-play defense that operates purely at test time. CleanSight (i) detects poisoned inputs based on the relative visual-text attention ratio in selected cross-modal fusion layers, and (ii) purifies the input by selectively pruning the suspicious high-attention visual tokens to neutralize the backdoor activation. Extensive experiments show that CleanSight significantly outperforms existing pixel-based purification defenses across diverse datasets and backdoor attack types, while preserving the model's utility on both clean and poisoned samples.

processed.summary:
• Identifies the mechanistic cause of **backdoor behaviors** in LVLMs as **attention stealing**, where triggers divert cross-modal attention from text to visual tokens.
• Introduces **CleanSight**, a training-free, test-time defense architecture that detects and purifies poisoned inputs without requiring model retraining.
• Utilizes **cross-modal attention ratio** monitoring to identify suspicious tokens and implements **selective token pruning** to neutralize backdoor activations.
• Demonstrates robust performance in preserving **model utility** while effectively mitigating diverse backdoor attack types across multiple datasets.
key_results: Discovered that backdoor triggers in LVLMs operate through attention redistribution rather than low-level visual patterns.
Achieved significant reduction in Attack Success Rate (ASR) compared to state-of-the-art pixel-based purification.
Maintained high model accuracy on clean datasets while defense mechanisms were active.
Validated the plug-and-play nature of CleanSight across various LVLM architectures and adapters.
Eliminated the need for computationally expensive retraining or clean data fine-tuning for backdoor mitigation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CleanSight to neutralize LVLM backdoors by pruning visual tokens that exhibit abnormal cross-modal attention stealing.

title: Retrieval-Enhanced Real Estate Appraisal
summary: The Sales Comparison Approach (SCA) is one of the most popular when it comes to real estate appraisal. Used as a reference in real estate expertise and as one of the major types of Automatic Valuation Models (AVM), it recently gained popularity within machine learning methods. The performance of models able to use data represented as sets and graphs made it possible to adapt this methodology efficiently, yielding substantial results. SCA relies on taking past transactions (comparables) as references, selected according to their similarity with the target property's sale. In this study, we focus on the selection of these comparables for real estate appraisal. We demonstrate that the selection of comparables used in many state-of-the-art algorithms can be significantly improved by learning a selection policy instead of imposing it. Our method relies on a hybrid vector-geographical retrieval module capable of adapting to different datasets and optimized jointly with an estimation module. We further show that the use of carefully selected comparables makes it possible to build models that require fewer comparables and fewer parameters with performance close to state-of-the-art models. All our evaluations are made on five datasets which span areas in the United States, Brazil, and France.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **hybrid vector-geographical retrieval module** that replaces static rules with a learned selection policy for identifying property comparables.
- The architecture **jointly optimizes** retrieval and estimation modules, allowing the system to adapt dynamically to diverse geographical datasets.
- Utilizes the **Sales Comparison Approach (SCA)** to build efficient models that require significantly fewer parameters than traditional state-of-the-art methods.
key_results: Learned selection policies significantly outperform fixed heuristic-based comparable selection in real estate tasks.
The model maintains state-of-the-art accuracy while using fewer transactions as references (comparables).
Joint optimization of retrieval and estimation modules enables higher adaptability across international datasets.
Hybrid retrieval successfully merges geometric/spatial data with feature-based vector similarity.
Architecture reduction leads to more lightweight models without sacrificing valuation performance.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a hybrid retrieval-estimation framework that improves real estate appraisal accuracy with fewer parameters and comparables.

title: Is Human Annotation Necessary? Iterative MBR Distillation for Error Span Detection in Machine Translation
summary: Error Span Detection (ESD) is a crucial subtask in Machine Translation (MT) evaluation, aiming to identify the location and severity of translation errors. While fine-tuning models on human-annotated data improves ESD performance, acquiring such data is expensive and prone to inconsistencies among annotators. To address this, we propose a novel self-evolution framework based on Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) decoding, named Iterative MBR Distillation for ESD, which eliminates the reliance on human annotations by leveraging an off-the-shelf LLM to generate pseudo-labels.Extensive experiments on the WMT Metrics Shared Task datasets demonstrate that models trained solely on these self-generated pseudo-labels outperform both unadapted base model and supervised baselines trained on human annotations at the system and span levels, while maintaining competitive sentence-level performance.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel **self-evolution framework** called Iterative MBR Distillation for **Error Span Detection (ESD)** in Machine Translation.
- Utilizes an off-the-shelf **Large Language Model (LLM)** to generate pseudo-labels, bypassing the need for expensive and inconsistent **human-annotated data**.
- Employs **Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) decoding** as a foundation for refining labels through iterative cycles of model distillation.
- Architecture focuses on identifying **translation error locations and severity** to improve automated system-level evaluation metrics.
key_results: Outperformed supervised baselines trained on human-annotated data at the system level.
Achieved superior performance in span-level detection compared to unadapted base models.
Maintained competitive sentence-level evaluation performance relative to state-of-the-art models.
Demonstrated successful cross-dataset generalization on WMT Metrics Shared Task benchmarks.
Validated LLM-based pseudo-labeling as a viable alternative to human expert annotation for ESD.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Zhejiang University researchers introduce an iterative distillation framework that leverages LLMs to automate high-accuracy machine translation error detection.

title: Generative Horcrux: Designing AI Carriers for Afterlife Selves
summary: As generative AI technologies rapidly advance, AI agents are gaining the ability not only to collect data and perform tasks but also to respond to environments and evolve over time. This shift opens new possibilities for reimagining digital legacy - raising critical questions about how we remember, commemorate, and interact with the traces of the deceased. The forms of these AI agents are particularly important, as they act as vessels for digital legacies - much like urns for ashes. We will ask: What kinds of devices or representations would we want to store our digital selves or legacies in? How do we envision future generations interacting with these forms? The question is not only about the function of these agents or the object's role as a storage vessel but also the meaning it carries, the memories it preserves, and its connection to the extended notion of our "Generative Horcrux." This three-hour in-person workshop invites design practitioners and researchers from diverse backgrounds to explore the emerging landscape of generative AI agent-based digital legacy. This workshop uses fiction and hands on prototyping to explore how AI agents might reconfigure memory, identity, and posthumous presence in future sociotechnical worlds. We anticipate that this session will foster interdisciplinary dialogue and contribute conceptually and methodologically to HCI, design research, and AI ethics.

processed.summary:
- Proposes an **agentic architecture** for digital legacy where AI models serve as evolving 'Generative Horcruxes' rather than static data archives.
- Utilizes **speculative design** and hands-on prototyping to develop physical and digital containers that manage long-term state and memory for posthumous AI agents.
- Evaluates the **HCI (Human-Computer Interaction)** impact of autonomous agents that simulate personality and evolve in response to future environments.
key_results: Conceptualization of AI agents as dynamic vessels for digital legacy storage.
Transition from static digital archives to evolving, behavioral-driven AI representations.
Framework for using speculative fiction in the design of agentic commemorative interfaces.
Exploration of the sociotechnical reconfigurations of identity and posthumous presence.
Development of interdisciplinary methodologies for HCI and AI ethics research.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose the 'Generative Horcrux' framework to design AI agents as evolving vessels for digital legacy and commemoration.

title: Deep Predictor-Corrector Networks for Robust Parameter Estimation in Non-autonomous System with Discontinuous Inputs
summary: Learning under non-smooth objectives remains a fundamental challenge in machine learning, as abrupt changes in conditioning variables can induce highly non-smooth loss landscapes and destabilize optimization. This difficulty is particularly pronounced in non-autonomous dynamical systems driven by discontinuous inputs, where widely used optimization methods, including recent neural smoothing approaches, exhibit unreliable convergence or strong hyperparameter sensitivity. To address this issue, we propose Deep Predictor-Corrector Networks (DePCoN), a multi-scale learning framework that stabilizes optimization by learning scale-consistent parameter update rules across a hierarchy of smoothed inputs. Rather than treating smoothing as a fixed preprocessing choice, DePCoN integrates smoothing into the learning dynamics itself through a learned predictor-corrector mechanism. Across biological and ecological benchmarks with discontinuous inputs, DePCoN consistently achieves more robust and faster convergence than existing methods while substantially reducing sensitivity to hyperparameter choices. Beyond dynamical systems, our approach provides a general learning principle for stabilizing optimization under non-smooth objectives.

processed.summary:
- **DePCoN** (Deep Predictor-Corrector Networks) introduces a multi-scale learning framework designed to stabilize optimization in non-autonomous dynamical systems featuring **discontinuous inputs**.
- The architecture utilizes a **learned predictor-corrector mechanism** to integrate smoothing directly into learning dynamics rather than as a static preprocessing step.
- The framework achieves **scale-consistent parameter update rules** across a hierarchy of smoothed inputs, mitigating the instability of highly non-smooth loss landscapes.
- Experimental results show significant improvements in **convergence speed** and robustness against hyperparameter sensitivity in complex biological and ecological models.
key_results: Development of the DePCoN architecture for handling non-smooth loss landscapes in dynamical systems.
Implementation of a hierarchy of smoothed inputs to ensure scale-consistent parameter updates.
Demonstrated superior convergence rates compared to traditional neural smoothing methods.
Significant reduction in sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning for discontinuous system inputs.
Validation across biological and ecological benchmarks showing robust and faster parameter estimation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DePCoN to stabilize optimization in non-smooth dynamical systems through a learned, multi-scale predictor-corrector mechanism.

title: Vocabulary aggregation
summary: A vocabulary is a list of words designating subsets from a grand set X. We model a vocabulary as a partition of X and study the aggregation of individual vocabularies into a collective one. We characterize aggregation rules when X is linearly ordered and each word of the vocabulary spans an order interval. We allow for individual vocabularies to differ both in the number and in the span of their words. Under a suitable restriction on agents' preferences, we show that our aggregation rules are strategy-proof.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a mathematical framework for **vocabulary aggregation**, modeling individual lexicons as partitions of a linearly ordered set where words represent contiguous intervals.
• Characterizes specific **aggregation rules** designed to consolidate disparate individual vocabularies into a single collective structure.
• Establishes **strategy-proofness** for these rules, ensuring that agents cannot manipulate the collective outcome by misrepresenting their individual partitions.
• Analyzes the impact of varying **word spans and counts**, allowing for a flexible model of how different agents categorize the same underlying data.
key_results: Formalization of vocabularies as interval-based partitions of a grand set.
Characterization of rules that effectively merge individual categorical boundaries.
Proof of strategy-proofness under restricted agent preference profiles.
Validation of the model for vocabularies with different cardinalities and spans.
Development of a theoretical basis for consensus-driven categorical systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers establish mathematical rules for aggregating individual word partitions into a strategy-proof, collective vocabulary for ordered datasets.

title: Delta1 with LLM: symbolic and neural integration for credible and explainable reasoning
summary: Neuro-symbolic reasoning increasingly demands frameworks that unite the formal rigor of logic with the interpretability of large language models (LLMs). We introduce an end to end explainability by construction pipeline integrating the Automated Theorem Generator Delta1 based on the full triangular standard contradiction (FTSC) with LLMs. Delta1 deterministically constructs minimal unsatisfiable clause sets and complete theorems in polynomial time, ensuring both soundness and minimality by construction. The LLM layer verbalizes each theorem and proof trace into coherent natural language explanations and actionable insights. Empirical studies across health care, compliance, and regulatory domains show that Delta1 and LLM enables interpretable, auditable, and domain aligned reasoning. This work advances the convergence of logic, language, and learning, positioning constructive theorem generation as a principled foundation for neuro-symbolic explainable AI.

processed.summary:
• Proposes an **explainability by construction** pipeline that bridges formal symbolic logic with neural language generation.
• Integrates the **Delta1 Automated Theorem Generator**, leveraging **Full Triangular Standard Contradiction (FTSC)** to produce sound, minimal theorems in polynomial time.
• Employs an **LLM layer** to verbalize deterministic symbolic proofs into human-readable, **auditable explanations** and actionable insights.
• Targets the **convergence of logic and learning**, providing a framework for high-stakes reasoning in regulatory and healthcare domains.
key_results: Deterministic construction of minimal unsatisfiable clause sets in polynomial time.
Elimination of hallucinations in the reasoning core through formal logic constraints.
Seamless translation of symbolic proof traces into coherent natural language.
Successful empirical validation across healthcare, compliance, and regulatory datasets.
Establishment of a sound foundation for neuro-symbolic, domain-aligned reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Delta1-LLM to bridge formal logic and natural language for verifiable, explainable neuro-symbolic reasoning.

title: Almost-Free Queue Jumping for Prior Inputs in Private Neural Inference
summary:
Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning as a Service (PP-MLaaS) enables secure neural network inference by integrating cryptographic primitives such as homomorphic encryption (HE) and multi-party computation (MPC), protecting both client data and server models. Recent mixed-primitive frameworks have significantly improved inference efficiency, yet they process batched inputs sequentially, offering little flexibility for prioritizing urgent requests. Naïve queue jumping introduces considerable computational and communication overhead, increasing non-negligible latency for in-queue inputs.
  We initiate the study of privacy-preserving queue jumping in batched inference and propose PrivQJ, a novel framework that enables efficient priority handling without degrading overall system performance. PrivQJ exploits shared computation across inputs via in-processing slot recycling, allowing prior inputs to be piggybacked onto ongoing batch computation with almost no additional cryptographic cost. Both theoretical analysis and experimental results demonstrate over an order-of-magnitude reduction in overhead compared to state-of-the-art PP-MLaaS systems.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **PrivQJ**, a framework designed for **privacy-preserving queue jumping** within batched neural network inference.
- Employs **in-processing slot recycling** to piggyback priority inputs onto ongoing **HE/MPC** (Homomorphic Encryption/Multi-Party Computation) computations.
- Addresses the sequential processing bottleneck in **PP-MLaaS**, allowing urgent requests to bypass the queue with negligible cryptographic cost.
- Maintains overall system throughput while preventing the latency penalties usually associated with naïve re-batching strategies.
key_results: Proposed the first dedicated framework for privacy-preserving priority handling in batched inference.
Achieved over an order-of-magnitude (10x+) reduction in overhead compared to state-of-the-art systems.
Demonstrated 'almost-free' priority insertion without increasing latency for existing in-queue inputs.
Validated theoretical gains through experimental results in secure neural inference environments.
Eliminated the need for expensive re-computation or communication when handling urgent client requests.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce PrivQJ to enable nearly zero-cost priority queueing in privacy-preserving neural network inference through slot recycling.

title: MotionAnymesh: Physics-Grounded Articulation for Simulation-Ready Digital Twins
summary: Converting static 3D meshes into interactable articulated assets is crucial for embodied AI and robotic simulation. However, existing zero-shot pipelines struggle with complex assets due to a critical lack of physical grounding. Specifically, ungrounded Vision-Language Models (VLMs) frequently suffer from kinematic hallucinations, while unconstrained joint estimation inevitably leads to catastrophic mesh inter-penetration during physical simulation. To bridge this gap, we propose MotionAnymesh, an automated zero-shot framework that seamlessly transforms unstructured static meshes into simulation-ready digital twins. Our method features a kinematic-aware part segmentation module that grounds VLM reasoning with explicit SP4D physical priors, effectively eradicating kinematic hallucinations. Furthermore, we introduce a geometry-physics joint estimation pipeline that combines robust type-aware initialization with physics-constrained trajectory optimization to rigorously guarantee collision-free articulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MotionAnymesh significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both geometric precision and dynamic physical executability, providing highly reliable assets for downstream applications.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **MotionAnymesh**, an automated zero-shot framework designed to transform unstructured static 3D meshes into **simulation-ready digital twins**.
- Implements a **kinematic-aware part segmentation** module that grounds VLM reasoning with **SP4D physical priors** to eliminate kinematic hallucinations.
- Utilizes a **geometry-physics joint estimation** pipeline featuring physics-constrained trajectory optimization to ensure **collision-free articulation**.
- Bridges the gap between vision-language reasoning and physical executability for **embodied AI** and robotic simulation environments.
key_results: Successfully eliminates kinematic hallucinations by grounding VLM reasoning in explicit physical priors.
Ensures rigorous collision-free movement through physics-constrained trajectory optimization.
Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in geometric precision and dynamic physical executability.
Provides a robust zero-shot solution for converting unstructured assets into interactive models.
Demonstrates high reliability for downstream applications in robotics and digital twin simulations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: MotionAnymesh researchers introduce a zero-shot framework that utilizes physical priors to transform static 3D meshes into simulation-ready articulated assets.

title: Can Fairness Be Prompted? Prompt-Based Debiasing Strategies in High-Stakes Recommendations
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) can infer sensitive attributes such as gender or age from indirect cues like names and pronouns, potentially biasing recommendations. While several debiasing methods exist, they require access to the LLMs' weights, are computationally costly, and cannot be used by lay users. To address this gap, we investigate implicit biases in LLM Recommenders (LLMRecs) and explore whether prompt-based strategies can serve as a lightweight and easy-to-use debiasing approach. We contribute three bias-aware prompting strategies for LLMRecs. To our knowledge, this is the first study on prompt-based debiasing approaches in LLMRecs that focuses on group fairness for users. Our experiments with 3 LLMs, 4 prompt templates, 9 sensitive attribute values, and 2 datasets show that our proposed debiasing approach, which instructs an LLM to be fair, can improve fairness by up to 74% while retaining comparable effectiveness, but might overpromote specific demographic groups in some cases.

processed.summary:
• The paper introduces three **bias-aware prompting strategies** designed to mitigate demographic bias in **LLM Recommender Systems (LLMRecs)** without requiring access to model weights.
• It investigates how **implicit biases** are triggered by indirect cues like names and pronouns, affecting **group fairness** across various sensitive attributes.
• The research demonstrates that **instructional debiasing** provides a lightweight, accessible alternative to computationally expensive fine-tuning for **fairness-aware recommendations**.
• The proposed architecture focuses on maintaining **recommendation effectiveness** while significantly reducing bias through specific prompt engineering techniques.
key_results: Improved group fairness in recommendations by up to 74% through prompt-based instructions.
Maintained comparable recommendation effectiveness (utility) despite stricter fairness constraints.
Confirmed that LLMs infer sensitive demographic attributes from indirect cues like names.
Evaluated robustness across 3 different LLMs and 4 distinct prompt templates.
Identified a risk of 'overpromotion' where debiasing can over-correct for specific demographic groups.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that instructional prompting can improve LLM recommendation fairness by 74% without compromising performance.

title: DS$^2$-Instruct: Domain-Specific Data Synthesis for Large Language Models Instruction Tuning
summary: Adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to specialized domains requires high-quality instruction tuning datasets, which are expensive to create through human annotation. Existing data synthesis methods focus on general-purpose tasks and fail to capture domain-specific terminology and reasoning patterns. To address this, we introduce DS$^2$-Instruct, a zero-shot framework that generates domain-specific instruction datasets without human supervision. Our approach first generates task-informed keywords to ensure comprehensive domain coverage. It then creates diverse instructions by pairing these keywords with different cognitive levels from Bloom's Taxonomy. Finally, it uses self-consistency validation to ensure data quality. We apply this framework to generate datasets across seven challenging domains, such as mathematics, finance, and logical reasoning. Comprehensive evaluation demonstrates that models fine-tuned on our generated data achieve substantial improvements over existing data generation methods.

processed.summary:
- DS2-Instruct provides a **zero-shot framework** for generating high-quality domain-specific instruction datasets without requiring manual human annotation.
- The system utilizes **task-informed keywords** paired with **Bloom's Taxonomy** to create a diverse range of instructions across varying cognitive complexity levels.
- A **self-consistency validation** step is implemented to filter synthetic data, ensuring the accuracy and reliability of the generated instruction-response pairs.
- The approach demonstrates superior performance in specialized fields like **finance, logic, and mathematics** compared to existing general-purpose synthesis methods.
key_results: Achieved significant performance improvements across seven specialized domains including finance and logic.
Successfully mapped domain-specific keywords to different cognitive levels for better dataset diversity.
Eliminated the need for human supervision in the instruction tuning data creation pipeline.
Developed a robust self-consistency mechanism that significantly improved synthetic data quality.
Outperformed existing data generation baselines in domain-specific benchmarks and reasoning tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DS2-Instruct, a zero-shot framework using Bloom’s Taxonomy to synthesize high-quality domain-specific instruction tuning data.

title: Teaching Agile Requirements Engineering: A Stakeholder Simulation with Generative AI
summary: Context: The active involvement of users and customers in agile software development remains a persistent challenge in practice. For this reason, it is important that students in higher education become familiar with good practices in Agile Requirements Engineering during their studies. Objective: Our objective is to enable students to learn how to interact with Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) through the use of a stakeholder simulation with AI Personas, while also developing an understanding of the limitations of AI tools in practical contexts. Method: In our courses, we employ a stakeholder simulation using GenAI, in which students conduct interviews with AI Personas through a provided meta-prompt. Based on the outcomes of these interviews, students apply agile practices (e.g., story mapping or impact mapping) to document requirements. The use of GenAI is subsequently reflected upon in a structured group discussion. Results: Through this approach, students gain practical experience by applying state-of-the art agile practices for requirements elicitation and documentation while simultaneously developing an understanding of the technical and ethical limitations associated with the use of generative AI. Conclusion: We have applied this approach over several terms and found that using a meta-prompt provides flexibility, allowing us to remain independent of specific large language model providers.

processed.summary:
- Implements a **stakeholder simulation** framework using **AI Personas** to facilitate Agile Requirements Engineering (RE) education.
- Utilizes a model-agnostic **meta-prompting** architecture to ensure flexibility across different Large Language Model providers.
- Integrates GenAI-driven interview outputs with **agile practices** such as story mapping and impact mapping for requirements documentation.
- Facilitates the evaluation of **technical and ethical limitations** of generative AI in the context of professional software development workflows.
key_results: Validation of meta-prompting as an effective method for maintaining LLM provider independence.
Successful simulation of stakeholder interviews using AI Personas for requirements elicitation.
Seamless integration of AI-generated content into agile workflows like impact mapping.
Demonstrated pedagogical success over multiple academic terms in higher education.
Development of student awareness regarding AI hallucinations and ethical constraints in software engineering.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers implement a meta-prompting framework using AI Personas to simulate stakeholder interviews for agile requirements engineering education.

title: GoalSwarm: Multi-UAV Semantic Coordination for Open-Vocabulary Object Navigation
summary: Cooperative visual semantic navigation is a foundational capability for aerial robot teams operating in unknown environments. However, achieving robust open-vocabulary object-goal navigation remains challenging due to the computational constraints of deploying heavy perception models onboard and the complexity of decentralized multi-agent coordination. We present GoalSwarm, a fully decentralized multi-UAV framework for zero-shot semantic object-goal navigation. Each UAV collaboratively constructs a shared, lightweight 2D top-down semantic occupancy map by projecting depth observations from aerial vantage points, eliminating the computational burden of full 3D representations while preserving essential geometric and semantic structure. The core contributions of GoalSwarm are threefold: (1) integration of zero-shot foundation model -- SAM3 for open vocabulary detection and pixel-level segmentation, enabling open-vocabulary target identification without task-specific training; (2) a Bayesian Value Map that fuses multi-viewpoint detection confidences into a per-pixel goal-relevance distribution, enabling informed frontier scoring via Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) exploration; and (3) a decentralized coordination strategy combining semantic frontier extraction, cost-utility bidding with geodesic path costs, and spatial separation penalties to minimize redundant exploration across the swarm.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **GoalSwarm**, a decentralized multi-UAV framework for zero-shot, **open-vocabulary object navigation** in unknown environments.
- Integrates **SAM3 foundation models** for pixel-level segmentation, enabling target identification without the need for task-specific training data.
- Implements a **Bayesian Value Map** and **UCB (Upper Confidence Bound)** exploration to probabilistically fuse multi-viewpoint detections and optimize frontier scoring.
- Utilizes a **cost-utility bidding** strategy with spatial separation penalties to ensure efficient, non-redundant exploration across the swarm.
key_results: Achieves zero-shot navigation to arbitrary objects via open-vocabulary detection.
Reduces onboard compute requirements by utilizing lightweight 2D semantic occupancy maps instead of 3D representations.
Enables robust multi-viewpoint confidence fusion using a Bayesian per-pixel distribution.
Optimizes swarm efficiency through decentralized geodesic path cost bidding.
Minimizes redundant exploration via spatial separation penalties in agent coordination.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce GoalSwarm, using SAM3 foundation models for decentralized, zero-shot open-vocabulary navigation in multi-UAV swarms.

title: Human-Centered Evaluation of an LLM-Based Process Modeling Copilot: A Mixed-Methods Study with Domain Experts
summary: Integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) into business process management tools promises to democratize Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) modeling for non-experts. While automated frameworks assess syntactic and semantic quality, they miss human factors like trust, usability, and professional alignment. We conducted a mixed-methods evaluation of our proposed solution, an LLM-powered BPMN copilot, with five process modeling experts using focus groups and standardized questionnaires. Our findings reveal a critical tension between acceptable perceived usability (mean CUQ score: 67.2/100) and notably lower trust (mean score: 48.8\%), with reliability rated as the most critical concern (M=1.8/5). Furthermore, we identified output-quality issues, prompting difficulties, and a need for the LLM to ask more in-depth clarifying questions about the process. We envision five use cases ranging from domain-expert support to enterprise quality assurance. We demonstrate the necessity of human-centered evaluation complementing automated benchmarking for LLM modeling agents.

processed.summary:
Introduces an **LLM-powered BPMN copilot** designed to democratize business process modeling through a conversational agent interface.
Employs a **mixed-methods evaluation framework** combining focus groups and standardized questionnaires to bridge the gap between automated benchmarks and human factors.
Reveals a critical **trust-usability disconnect**, where technical usability scores (67.2/100) significantly outperform expert trust levels (48.8%).
Advocates for **reasoning-centric agent architectures** that utilize proactive clarifying questions to resolve domain ambiguities in complex workflows.
key_results: Usability was rated at a mean CUQ score of 67.2/100, suggesting fair user-facing performance.
Mean trust score was significantly lower at 48.8%, indicating skepticism from domain experts.
Reliability was identified as the weakest performance factor, scoring only 1.8 out of 5.
Experts identified prompt engineering as a major friction point for non-technical business users.
Human-centered metrics provided insights into professional alignment that automated syntactic checks missed.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLM process modeling agents face a significant trust deficit despite maintaining acceptable usability scores.

title: Dynamic analysis of refractive index evolution and diffraction properties during single-photon polymerization of photopolymers for micro-optical applications
summary: Photopolymerization enables the production of micro-optical elements, such as diffractive optical elements or GRIN optics. This process utilizes targeted spatial modulation of the refractive index, which is achieved through additive manufacturing. In this context, a thorough understanding of the dynamic processes during curing is essential in order to be able to accurately predict the optical function of the element. For this reason, this work investigates the kinetics and resulting optical properties of an acrylate-based photopolymer under UV irradiation using a DLP projection system. The experimental approach combines two measurement methods: On the one hand, the absolute change in the refractive index is determined in a time-resolved manner at the interface of a prism using a method based on total reflection. On the other hand, the formation of a phase grating in the volume of the polymer is monitored in real time by analyzing the diffraction orders of a coherent sample laser. The results show a characteristic S-shaped curve of the refractive index change, which reflects the phases of polymerization: oxygen inhibition, autoacceleration, and vitrification. Analysis of the diffraction patterns reveals complex intensity curves and substructures in the diffraction orders. These could be traced back experimentally and through simulations to the discrete pixel structure of the DLP projector and the existing dead zones. Furthermore, the simulation model developed on the basis of Fourier optics reproduces the experimental diffraction patterns and confirms the hypothesis that scattered light and radical diffusion lead to time-delayed polymerization in the theoretically unexposed areas. This results in a reduction of the refractive index contrast over time. This work thus provides parameters for the simulation and optimization of exposure strategies in 3D printing of micro-optics.

processed.summary:
* The research presents a dual-measurement approach to characterize **acrylate-based photopolymer kinetics** specifically for DLP-based 3D printing of micro-optics.
* It introduces a **Fourier optics-based simulation model** that successfully predicts diffraction patterns by accounting for pixel structures, scattered light, and radical diffusion.
* The study identifies three distinct phases of polymerization—**oxygen inhibition, autoacceleration, and vitrification**—using time-resolved refractive index monitoring.
* This framework enables the **optimization of exposure strategies** to minimize loss of refractive index contrast in additive manufacturing.
key_results: Identification of an S-shaped refractive index evolution curve corresponding to polymerization stages.
Discovery that scattered light and radical diffusion cause time-delayed polymerization in unexposed zones.
Successful experimental validation of complex diffraction substructures using a pixel-aware simulation model.
Quantification of the refractive index contrast reduction over time due to chemical diffusion.
Establishment of parameters for accurate predictive modeling of diffractive optical element manufacturing.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers develop a Fourier optics simulation model to optimize refractive index precision in 3D-printed micro-optical elements.

title: Enhanced Drug-drug Interaction Prediction Using Adaptive Knowledge Integration
summary: Drug-drug interaction event (DDIE) prediction is crucial for preventing adverse reactions and ensuring optimal therapeutic outcomes. However, existing methods often face challenges with imbalanced datasets, complex interaction mechanisms, and poor generalization to unknown drug combinations. To address these challenges, we propose a knowledge augmentation framework that adaptively infuses prior drug knowledge into a large language model (LLM). This framework utilizes reinforcement learning techniques to facilitate adaptive knowledge extraction and synthesis, thereby efficiently optimizing the strategy space to enhance the accuracy of LLMs for DDIE predictions. As a result of few-shot learning, we achieved a notable improvement compared to the baseline. This approach establishes an effective framework for scientific knowledge learning for DDIE predictions.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **knowledge augmentation framework** that adaptively infuses structured drug information into a **Large Language Model (LLM)** for interaction prediction.
- Utilizes **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** to optimize the strategy space for knowledge extraction and synthesis, enhancing the model's reasoning capabilities.
- Focuses on overcoming data challenges like **imbalanced datasets** and poor generalization to unseen or unknown drug combinations.
- Demonstrates the effectiveness of **few-shot learning** in specialized scientific domains, providing a scalable template for knowledge-heavy AI tasks.
key_results: Achieved notable accuracy improvements in DDIE prediction over existing baselines.
Successfully implemented adaptive knowledge extraction using RL-driven strategies.

title: Test-time RL alignment exposes task familiarity artifacts in LLM benchmarks
summary: Direct evaluation of LLMs on benchmarks can be misleading because comparatively strong performance may reflect task familiarity rather than capability. The train-before-test approach controls for task familiarity by giving each model task-relevant training before evaluation, originally through supervised finetuning. However, suitable training data is often hard to come by, and evaluation results vary with the data chosen. In this paper, we propose a two-stage test-time reinforcement learning (RL) alignment method for train-before-test. First, RL with a single sample provides a first alignment of the model to the task format, and second, test-time RL with majority-voting reward aligns the model to the benchmark distribution. Our test-time RL alignment method aligns similarly well as SFT-based train-before test, but without requiring a task-specific training set. On a domain-specific benchmark without training data, we show that direct evaluation underestimates base models which perform substantially better once aligned, yielding a more faithful evaluation of their capabilities. Moreover, for reasoning tasks, the performance gap between fine-tuned models and their base models largely disappears after alignment, suggesting that many gains from RLVR/SFT reported in the literature are not a difference in reasoning capability, but rather artifacts of task familiarity.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **two-stage test-time RL alignment** framework for the 'train-before-test' evaluation paradigm to distinguish innate capability from task familiarity.
- Utilizes **single-sample RL** for initial task format alignment followed by **majority-voting reward RL** to align the model to the specific benchmark distribution.
- Eliminates the need for **task-specific training sets** or SFT data, allowing for more faithful evaluation of base models on domain-specific benchmarks.
- Challenges the perceived superiority of fine-tuned models by showing that **reasoning performance gaps** often vanish once base models are properly aligned at test-time.
key_results: Test-time RL alignment achieves performance parity with SFT-based methods without requiring curated training data.
Direct evaluation significantly underestimates base model capabilities on domain-specific tasks.
The performance gap between SFT/RLVR fine-tuned models and base models largely disappears after test-time alignment.
Majority-voting rewards effectively guide models to converge on benchmark-specific distributions.
Identifies that many reported gains in reasoning benchmarks are artifacts of task familiarity rather than logic improvements.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that test-time RL alignment exposes benchmark gains as task familiarity artifacts rather than reasoning improvements.

title: CLARIN-PT-LDB: An Open LLM Leaderboard for Portuguese to assess Language, Culture and Civility
summary: This paper reports on the development of a leaderboard of Open Large Language Models (LLM) for European Portuguese (PT-PT), and on its associated benchmarks. This leaderboard comes as a way to address a gap in the evaluation of LLM for European Portuguese, which so far had no leaderboard dedicated to this variant of the language. The paper also reports on novel benchmarks, including some that address aspects of performance that so far have not been available in benchmarks for European Portuguese, namely model safeguards and alignment to Portuguese culture. The leaderboard is available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/PORTULAN/portuguese-llm-leaderboard.

• Implements an **automated scoring pipeline** hosted on Hugging Face Spaces to provide a transparent, reproducible comparison of open-source models for the Portuguese research community.

title: Surrogates for Physics-based and Data-driven Modelling of Parametric Systems: Review and New Perspectives
summary: Surrogate models provide compact relations between user-defined input parameters and output quantities of interest, enabling the efficient evaluation of complex parametric systems in many-query settings. Such capabilities are essential in a wide range of applications, including optimisation, control, data assimilation, uncertainty quantification, and emerging digital twin technologies in various fields such as manufacturing, personalised healthcare, smart cities, and sustainability. This article reviews established methodologies for constructing surrogate models exploiting either knowledge of the governing laws and the dynamical structure of the system (physics-based) or experimental observations (data-driven), as well as hybrid approaches combining these two paradigms. By revisiting the design of a surrogate model as a functional approximation problem, existing methodologies are reviewed in terms of the choice of (i) a reduced basis and (ii) a suitable approximation criterion. The paper reviews methodologies pertaining to the field of Scientific Machine Learning, and it aims at synthesising established knowledge, recent advances, and new perspectives on: dimensionality reduction, physics-based, and data-driven surrogate modelling based on proper orthogonal decomposition, proper generalised decomposition, and artificial neural networks; multi-fidelity methods to exploit information from sources with different fidelities; adaptive sampling, enrichment, and data augmentation techniques to enhance the quality of surrogate models.

processed.summary:
- Reviews the integration of **Scientific Machine Learning (SciML)** with traditional physics-based models to create efficient surrogate architectures for complex parametric systems.
- Explores **dimensionality reduction** techniques such as **Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD)** and **Proper Generalised Decomposition (PGD)** to handle high-dimensional input spaces.
- Evaluates the transition from purely data-driven **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)** to hybrid models that incorporate **governing laws** for improved generalization and physical consistency.
- Discusses **multi-fidelity methods** and **adaptive sampling** strategies that optimize the trade-off between computational cost and model accuracy in digital twin applications.
key_results: Comprehensive classification of surrogate models based on reduced basis selection and approximation criteria.
Validation of hybrid physics-data paradigms for more robust parametric system modeling.
Identification of multi-fidelity information fusion as a key driver for efficient data assimilation.
Advancement of adaptive enrichment techniques to enhance surrogate quality in many-query settings.
Strategic framework for implementing surrogate-based optimization and uncertainty quantification.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers review Scientific ML frameworks for surrogate modeling, enabling efficient digital twins through physics-data hybrid architectures.

title: Quantifying Perovskite Solar Cell Degradation via Machine Learning from Spatially Resolved Multimodal Luminescence Time Series
summary: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have experienced a remarkable rise in power conversion efficiency (PCE) over the past 15 years, positioning them as a promising alternative or complement to silicon for large-scale photovoltaic deployment. However, beyond scalable fabrication, operational stability remains a major bottleneck for commercialization. Reliable and rapid methods to assess device health and degradation mechanisms - ideally compatible with field applications - are therefore essential. We present a deep-learning framework to estimate efficiency retention, $R_\mathrm{PCE}=\mathrm{PCE}_t/\mathrm{PCE}_0$, directly from multimodal luminescence imaging acquired during device aging. Each training sample includes electroluminescence (EL), open-circuit photoluminescence (PLoc), and short-circuit photoluminescence (PLsc) images at an aged state, together with device-specific reference images at $t=0$. This design enables the model to learn spatially resolved degradation patterns relative to the pristine condition. The dataset was collected over 5-70 hours using an automated, in-house measurement platform. We introduce LumPerNet, a compact convolutional neural network that regresses $R_\mathrm{PCE}$ from stacked multimodal image tensors, and benchmark it against an intensity-only multilayer perceptron baseline. Using a leakage-aware protocol with device-level hold-out testing and four-fold cross-validation, restricted to $R_\mathrm{PCE}\in[0.8,1.2]$, LumPerNet achieves substantially improved and more robust performance (MAE -23.4%, RMSE -25.6%, $R^2$ +0.417). Ablation studies highlight the importance of complementary physical contrast across modalities for generalization. Overall, this work establishes a reproducible pipeline linking automated luminescence imaging to electrical labels, enabling accelerated stability testing and non-invasive degradation monitoring in PSCs.

processed.summary:
- The researchers introduce **LumPerNet**, a compact **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** designed to regress efficiency retention in Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) from multimodal image tensors.
- The model leverages **spatially resolved degradation patterns** by processing stacked electroluminescence (EL) and photoluminescence (PL) images relative to pristine state reference images.
- This framework establishes an **automated, non-invasive pipeline** for monitoring device health, effectively bridging the gap between high-resolution luminescence imaging and electrical performance metrics.
- The architecture emphasizes **multimodal fusion**, demonstrating that physical contrast across different imaging modes is critical for generalizing across various degradation mechanisms.
key_results: Achieved a 23.4% reduction in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) compared to the MLP baseline.
Reduced Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by 25.6% during efficiency retention regression.
Increased R-squared metric by 0.417, showing significantly improved model robustness.
Validated the necessity of multimodal data (EL, PLoc, and PLsc) through detailed ablation studies.
Successfully predicted efficiency retention within the 0.8 to 1.2 range using a device-level hold-out protocol.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers developed LumPerNet, a multimodal CNN that accurately predicts solar cell degradation using spatially resolved luminescence imaging.

title: On Linear Separability of the MNIST Handwritten Digits Dataset
summary: The MNIST dataset containing thousands of handwritten digit images is still a fundamental benchmark for evaluating various pattern-recognition and image-classification models. Linear separability is a key concept in many statistical and machine-learning techniques. Despite the long history of the MNIST dataset and its relative simplicity in size and resolution, the question of whether the dataset is linearly separable has never been fully answered -- scientific and informal sources share conflicting claims. This paper aims to provide a comprehensive empirical investigation to address this question, distinguishing pairwise and one-vs-rest separation of the training, the test and the combined sets, respectively. It reviews the theoretical approaches to assessing linear separability, alongside state-of-the-art methods and tools, then systematically examines all relevant assemblies, and reports the findings.

processed.summary:
- The paper investigates the fundamental **linear separability** of the MNIST dataset, addressing long-standing conflicting claims in machine learning literature.
- Researchers employ **empirical investigation** methods to test both pairwise and one-vs-rest separation across training, test, and combined data splits.
- The methodology involves a systematic review of **theoretical assessment tools** and modern optimization techniques to determine the geometric properties of the image manifold.
- The findings establish a baseline for **model complexity** requirements when designing image-classification architectures for handwritten digit recognition.
key_results: Comprehensive empirical determination of linear separability for all digit classes.
Distinction between pairwise and one-vs-rest separation outcomes.
Rigorous testing of the combined training and test sets for global separability.
Identification of specific digit pairs that challenge linear classification boundaries.
Systematic documentation of state-of-the-art tools for assessing dataset geometry.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers conduct a comprehensive empirical study to resolve conflicting claims regarding the linear separability of the MNIST dataset.

title: Team LEYA in 10th ABAW Competition: Multimodal Ambivalence/Hesitancy Recognition Approach
summary: Ambivalence/hesitancy recognition in unconstrained videos is a challenging problem due to the subtle, multimodal, and context-dependent nature of this behavioral state. In this paper, a multimodal approach for video-level ambivalence/hesitancy recognition is presented for the 10th ABAW Competition. The proposed approach integrates four complementary modalities: scene, face, audio, and text. Scene dynamics are captured with a VideoMAE-based model, facial information is encoded through emotional frame-level embeddings aggregated by statistical pooling, acoustic representations are extracted with EmotionWav2Vec2.0 and processed by a Mamba-based temporal encoder, and linguistic cues are modeled using fine-tuned transformer-based text models. The resulting unimodal embeddings are further combined using multimodal fusion models, including prototype-augmented variants. Experiments on the BAH corpus demonstrate clear gains of multimodal fusion over all unimodal baselines. The best unimodal configuration achieved an average MF1 of 70.02%, whereas the best multimodal fusion model reached 83.25%. The highest final test performance, 71.43%, was obtained by an ensemble of five prototype-augmented fusion models. The obtained results highlight the importance of complementary multimodal cues and robust fusion strategies for ambivalence/hesitancy recognition.

processed.summary:
• The proposed architecture utilizes a **four-modality framework** integrating scene (VideoMAE), face (frame-level embeddings), audio (EmotionWav2Vec2.0), and text (Transformers) to detect subtle behavioral states.
• Acoustic processing is enhanced via a **Mamba-based temporal encoder**, leveraging state-space models for efficient sequence modeling of emotional audio cues.
• The system implements **prototype-augmented multimodal fusion**, which uses learned prototypes to anchor and align disparate unimodal embeddings for improved classification accuracy.
• Experimental results confirm that **multimodal ensembles** significantly outperform unimodal baselines, highlighting the necessity of cross-modal context for detecting complex states like hesitancy.
key_results: Multimodal fusion achieved an 83.25% MF1 score, significantly outperforming the best unimodal baseline of 70.02%.
An ensemble of five prototype-augmented fusion models yielded the highest final test performance of 71.43%.
Mamba-based temporal encoders were successfully integrated to process EmotionWav2Vec2.0 acoustic representations.
The VideoMAE-based scene dynamics model proved essential for capturing environmental context-dependent behavior.
Statistical pooling of facial emotional frame-level embeddings provided robust visual representations for fusion.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Team LEYA utilizes VideoMAE and Mamba-based fusion to improve multimodal ambivalence recognition in unconstrained video environments.

title: Multimodal Protein Language Models for Enzyme Kinetic Parameters: From Substrate Recognition to Conformational Adaptation
summary: Predicting enzyme kinetic parameters quantifies how efficiently an enzyme catalyzes a specific substrate under defined biochemical conditions. Canonical parameters such as the turnover number ($k_\text{cat}$), Michaelis constant ($K_\text{m}$), and inhibition constant ($K_\text{i}$) depend jointly on the enzyme sequence, the substrate chemistry, and the conformational adaptation of the active site during binding. Many learning pipelines simplify this process to a static compatibility problem between the enzyme and substrate, fusing their representations through shallow operations and regressing a single value. Such formulations overlook the staged nature of catalysis, which involves both substrate recognition and conformational adaptation. In this regard, we reformulate kinetic prediction as a staged multimodal conditional modeling problem and introduce the Enzyme-Reaction Bridging Adapter (ERBA), which injects cross-modal information via fine-tuning into Protein Language Models (PLMs) while preserving their biochemical priors. ERBA performs conditioning in two stages: Molecular Recognition Cross-Attention (MRCA) first injects substrate information into the enzyme representation to capture specificity; Geometry-aware Mixture-of-Experts (G-MoE) then integrates active-site structure and routes samples to pocket-specialized experts to reflect induced fit. To maintain semantic fidelity, Enzyme-Substrate Distribution Alignment (ESDA) enforces distributional consistency within the PLM manifold in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space. Experiments across three kinetic endpoints and multiple PLM backbones, ERBA delivers consistent gains and stronger out-of-distribution performance compared with sequence-only and shallow-fusion baselines, offering a biologically grounded route to scalable kinetic prediction and a foundation for adding cofactors, mutations, and time-resolved structural cues.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the **Enzyme-Reaction Bridging Adapter (ERBA)**, a staged multimodal framework that fine-tunes **Protein Language Models (PLMs)** by incorporating chemical and structural data.
- Implements **Molecular Recognition Cross-Attention (MRCA)** to capture enzyme-substrate specificity and **Geometry-aware Mixture-of-Experts (G-MoE)** to model conformational induced-fit adaptations.
- Utilizes **Enzyme-Substrate Distribution Alignment (ESDA)** to preserve the biological priors of the PLM manifold using a reproducing kernel Hilbert space approach.
- Provides a scalable foundation for predicting **kinetic parameters** like kcat, Km, and Ki by moving beyond simple shallow-fusion architectures.
key_results: Achieved consistent performance gains across three major kinetic endpoints: turnover number, Michaelis constant, and inhibition constant.
Demonstrated significantly stronger out-of-distribution performance compared to traditional sequence-only and shallow-fusion baselines.
Successfully validated the architecture across multiple diverse PLM backbones, proving its versatility as a model-agnostic adapter.
Shown that staged conditioning—separating recognition from adaptation—more accurately reflects the biological stages of catalysis.
Integrated 3D active-site structural cues effectively through specialized routing in the G-MoE component.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ERBA to improve enzyme kinetic predictions by integrating substrate chemistry and active-site geometry into PLMs.

title: Serving Hybrid LLM Loads with SLO Guarantees Using CPU-GPU Attention Piggybacking
summary:
Nowadays, service providers often deploy multiple types of LLM services within shared clusters. While the service colocation improves resource utilization, it introduces significant interference risks for latency-sensitive (LS) services-which have strict SLO requirements for inference latency-and severely constrain the service capacity of best-effort (BE) services due to limited available memory. To address interference, existing systems typically rely on reserving headroom to constrain BE resource usage. However, this approach's coarse granularity compromises the SLO compliance of the latency-sensitive service and unnecessarily restricts the generation potential of the best effort service.
  In this paper, we propose OmniServe, a novel LLM serving system that efficiently harnesses both CPU and GPU resources to mitigate interference and improve throughput. Central to OmniServe is the Attention Piggybacking mechanism, which effectively offloads the Attention computation of BE services to CPUs on the fly. This mechanism also facilitates asynchronous communication between CPU and GPU streams, preventing GPUs from being blocked while aggregating Attention results. Additionally, OmniServe incorporates a dynamic batching control policy to adapt to fluctuating request arrivals, facilitating Dense module computation using layer-wise batching. Experimental results show that OmniServe improves the SLO attainment rate for LS services by up to $1.48\times$ while enhancing BE serving throughput by up to $9.85\times$ compared to state-of-the-art systems.

processed.summary:
* OmniServe introduces a **hybrid CPU-GPU orchestration** framework designed to manage mixed latency-sensitive (LS) and best-effort (BE) LLM inference workloads.
* The core **Attention Piggybacking** mechanism dynamically offloads attention operations to the CPU to minimize interference on critical GPU paths for latency-sensitive tasks.
* Implements **asynchronous result aggregation** and **layer-wise batching** to maintain high GPU utilization for dense computations while preventing processing blocks.
key_results: Improves SLO attainment rate for latency-sensitive services by 1.48x.
Increases best-effort service throughput by up to 9.85x compared to state-of-the-art systems.
Successfully mitigates resource interference in shared clusters by utilizing idle CPU capacity.
Facilitates asynchronous communication between CPU and GPU streams to maximize hardware parallelism.
Optimizes Dense module computation through a novel dynamic batching control policy.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: OmniServe researchers introduce Attention Piggybacking to optimize hybrid CPU-GPU serving, significantly boosting inference SLO compliance and throughput.

title: coDrawAgents: A Multi-Agent Dialogue Framework for Compositional Image Generation
summary: Text-to-image generation has advanced rapidly, but existing models still struggle with faithfully composing multiple objects and preserving their attributes in complex scenes. We propose coDrawAgents, an interactive multi-agent dialogue framework with four specialized agents: Interpreter, Planner, Checker, and Painter that collaborate to improve compositional generation. The Interpreter adaptively decides between a direct text-to-image pathway and a layout-aware multi-agent process. In the layout-aware mode, it parses the prompt into attribute-rich object descriptors, ranks them by semantic salience, and groups objects with the same semantic priority level for joint generation. Guided by the Interpreter, the Planner adopts a divide-and-conquer strategy, incrementally proposing layouts for objects with the same semantic priority level while grounding decisions in the evolving visual context of the canvas. The Checker introduces an explicit error-correction mechanism by validating spatial consistency and attribute alignment, and refining layouts before they are rendered. Finally, the Painter synthesizes the image step by step, incorporating newly planned objects into the canvas to provide richer context for subsequent iterations. Together, these agents address three key challenges: reducing layout complexity, grounding planning in visual context, and enabling explicit error correction. Extensive experiments on benchmarks GenEval and DPG-Bench demonstrate that coDrawAgents substantially improves text-image alignment, spatial accuracy, and attribute binding compared to existing methods.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **coDrawAgents**, an interactive **multi-agent dialogue framework** consisting of four specialized agents (Interpreter, Planner, Checker, and Painter) to enhance **compositional image generation**.
- Features an **Interpreter** that adaptively selects between direct generation and a **layout-aware multi-agent process** based on prompt complexity and semantic priority levels.
- Employs a **divide-and-conquer strategy** where the Planner grounds decisions in evolving visual context, while the **Checker** provides an explicit error-correction loop for spatial and attribute consistency.
- Addresses key bottlenecks in **Text-to-Image (T2I)** synthesis, specifically reducing layout complexity and improving **attribute binding** for multiple objects in a single scene.
key_results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the GenEval benchmark for compositional generation.
Significant improvements in spatial accuracy and text-image alignment on DPG-Bench.
Demonstrated superior attribute binding, effectively preserving unique traits for multiple objects.
Validated the effectiveness of semantic priority grouping in reducing layout complexity.
Proved that explicit error-correction via a Checker agent significantly reduces rendering artifacts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce coDrawAgents, a multi-agent framework that uses collaborative reasoning to significantly improve compositional text-to-image accuracy.

title: Are Dependent Types in Set Theory Feasible?
summary: Following the types-as-sets paradigm, we present a mechanized embedding of dependent function types with a hierarchy of universes into schematic first-order logic with equality, with axiom schemas of Tarski-Grothendieck set theory. We carry this embedding in the Lisa proof assistant. On top of this foundation, we implement a proof-producing bidirectional type-checking tactic to compute proofs for typing judgements, with partial support for subtyping. We present examples showing how our approach enables automated reasoning for dependent types that is fully verified from set-theoretic axioms and deduction rules for schematic first-order logic with equality. Because types are merely sets, the resulting formalism supports equality that applies to all types and values and permits the usual substitution rules.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a mechanized **embedding of dependent function types** and a universe hierarchy into schematic first-order logic using **Tarski-Grothendieck (TG) set theory** axioms.
 • Implements a **proof-producing bidirectional type-checking tactic** within the Lisa proof assistant to automate the generation of formal proofs for typing judgements.
 • Leverages the **types-as-sets paradigm** to enable a unified formalism where set-theoretic equality and substitution rules apply universally to all types and values.
key_results: Successfully embedded dependent function types into schematic first-order logic with equality.
Integrated a hierarchy of universes compatible with Tarski-Grothendieck axioms.
Developed an automated bidirectional type-checking tactic for the Lisa proof assistant.
Demonstrated partial support for subtyping within a unified set-theoretic framework.
Enabled fully verified automated reasoning for dependent types starting from fundamental axioms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: EPFL researchers implement a mechanized embedding of dependent types in set theory to enable verified automated reasoning in Lisa.

title: Rethinking Multiple-Choice Questions for RLVR: Unlocking Potential via Distractor Design
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) significantly enhances the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models. When applied to RLVR, Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs) offer a scalable source of verifiable data but risk inducing reward hacking, where models shortcut reasoning via random guessing or simple elimination. Current approaches often mitigate this by converting MCQs to open-ended formats, thereby discarding the contrastive signal provided by expert-designed distractors. In this work, we systematically investigate the impact of option design on RLVR. Our analysis highlights two primary insights: (1) Mismatches in option counts between training and testing degrade performance. (2) Strong distractors effectively mitigate random guessing, enabling effective RLVR training even with 2-way questions. Motivated by these findings, we propose Iterative Distractor Curation (IDC), a framework that actively constructs high-quality distractors to block elimination shortcuts and promote deep reasoning. Experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate that our method effectively enhances distractor quality and yields significant gains in RLVR training compared to the original data.

processed.summary:
- Investigates **Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)** to improve LLM reasoning using Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs).
- Identifies **reward hacking** through random guessing and elimination shortcuts as a major barrier to effective RLVR training.
- Proposes **Iterative Distractor Curation (IDC)**, a framework that constructs high-quality distractors to force models into deeper reasoning paths.
- Demonstrates that **distractor quality** and matching option counts between train and test phases are critical for model stability.
key_results: Mismatches in the number of options between training and testing significantly degrade LLM reasoning performance.
Strong distractors effectively prevent models from using elimination shortcuts, enabling RLVR even on 2-way questions.
Converting MCQs to open-ended formats is counterproductive as it discards valuable contrastive signals from experts.
The IDC framework consistently produces higher-quality distractors than static, original datasets.
RLVR models trained with IDC distractors show significant gains in reasoning accuracy across multiple standard benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Iterative Distractor Curation to prevent reward hacking and enhance reasoning during RLVR training using MCQs.

title: Adaptive Vision-Language Model Routing for Computer Use Agents
summary: Computer Use Agents (CUAs) translate natural-language instructions into Graphical User Interface (GUI) actions such as clicks, keystrokes, and scrolls by relying on a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to interpret screenshots and predict grounded tool calls. However, grounding accuracy varies dramatically across VLMs, while current CUA systems typically route every action to a single fixed model regardless of difficulty. We propose \textbf{Adaptive VLM Routing} (AVR), a framework that inserts a lightweight semantic routing layer between the CUA orchestrator and a pool of VLMs. For each tool call, AVR estimates action difficulty from multimodal embeddings, probes a small VLM to measure confidence, and routes the action to the cheapest model whose predicted accuracy satisfies a target reliability threshold. For \textit{warm} agents with memory of prior UI interactions, retrieved context further narrows the capability gap between small and large models, allowing many actions to be handled without escalation. We formalize routing as a cost--accuracy trade-off, derive a threshold-based policy for model selection, and evaluate AVR using ScreenSpot-Pro grounding data together with the OpenClaw agent routing benchmark. Across these settings, AVR projects inference cost reductions of up to 78\% while staying within 2 percentage points of an all-large-model baseline. When combined with the Visual Confused Deputy guardrail, AVR also escalates high-risk actions directly to the strongest available model, unifying efficiency and safety within a single routing framework. Materials are also provided Model, benchmark, and code: https://github.com/vllm-project/semantic-router.

- Introduces **Adaptive VLM Routing (AVR)**, a framework for Computer Use Agents (CUAs) that dynamically routes GUI actions to a pool of VLMs based on estimated task difficulty.
- Utilizes **multimodal embeddings** and a small VLM probe to determine if an action requires escalation, optimizing the **cost-accuracy trade-off** for grounded tool calls.
- Leverages **retrieved context** from prior UI interactions to boost small model performance, allowing many actions to be handled locally without escalating to frontier models.
- Integrates a **Visual Confused Deputy guardrail** to automatically escalate high-risk actions to the strongest model, unifying safety and inference efficiency.

Maintained performance within 2 percentage points of a state-of-the-art all-large-model configuration.
Validated effectiveness on the ScreenSpot-Pro grounding dataset and OpenClaw agent routing benchmark.
Demonstrated that 'warm' agents with memory significantly narrow the capability gap between small and large VLMs.
Successfully implemented a threshold-based policy for model selection based on predicted accuracy and reliability.
